Reviewed by Claude A, 2026-07-18; native-confirmed rejected 2026-07-18 (Thangseng, via Tridip relay, in response to NV-019). Direct quote: "Wind is 'balwa'. The bal has a totally different meaning. It means load or burden." Confirms Bal != wind; Balwa is the correct and only word for wind. This also raises a new question about the other senses this dictionary source listed under the same 'Bal' headword (flower, air, big basket) - Thangseng's framing ('a totally different meaning', singular) suggests the printed source may have compressed multiple unrelated homonyms under one headword, same pattern as the Grika precedent. See NV-020.

Reviewed by Claude A, 2026-07-18. Flagged as within-batch conflict against Balgaoa. Not a real conflict - Bala is a broadly polysemous general verb (bloom/blow/carry in mouth/dress up/spread odour) where 'blossom' is one of many extended senses; Balgaoa is a dedicated flowering verb. Near-synonym pair, both legitimate.

Near-synonym pair, both legitimate.

english: It is used as numeral pre-fix for flower and bundle.
notes: [OCR-flagged for review: ocr_confidence=High] Separate entry for 'Bal'. POS tag is noun but the definition is a full explanatory sentence.
review_notes: Reviewed by Claude A, 2026-07-18 (page 18 backfill). Not a translatable sense - this is a grammatical/usage note (Bal functioning as a numeral classifier/prefix for flower and bundle), not something anyone would search as English text to translate. Doesn't belong in the flat english->garo dictionary. Worth documenting as a classifier note elsewhere, not rejecting the underlying observation, just this import shape.
